content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 214–215 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_017_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_017_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### Offerings Must Be Without Defect (v. 1)

**1** Do not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep that has any defect or serious flaw — that is detestable to the LORD your God.

---

### Idolaters Must Be Tried and Executed (vv. 2–7)

**2** Suppose it is reported to you in one of the towns the LORD your God is giving you that a man or woman among you has done evil in the sight of the LORD your God and violated his covenant — **3** going to worship other gods, bowing down to them, to the sun or the moon or the stars of the sky, which I have forbidden. **4** When you hear about it, investigate it thoroughly. If the report is true and proven — if this detestable thing has really happened in Israel — **5** take that man or woman who has done this evil thing to your town gate and stone them to death. **6** No one may be put to death on the testimony of only one witness. There must be two or three witnesses. **7** The hands of the witnesses must be the first raised against the condemned to execute them, and then the whole community follows. You must purge the evil from among you.

---

### The Supreme Court — Bring Hard Cases to the Priests (vv. 8–13)

**8** When cases come before you that are too difficult for your local courts to decide — matters involving bloodshed, lawsuits, or assaults — take them to the place the LORD your God will choose. **9** Go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is serving at that time. Inquire of them and they will give you the verdict. **10** You must carry out exactly the verdict they announce at the place the LORD will choose, and be careful to do everything they instruct you. **11** Act in accordance with whatever ruling they give you and with every judgment they hand down. Do not turn aside from what they tell you — neither to the right nor to the left. **12** Anyone who shows contempt for the judge or for the priest who stands ministering there before the LORD your God is to be put to death. You must purge the evil from Israel. **13** All the people will hear and be afraid, and no one will dare to be contemptuous again.

---

### Instructions for the King — When You Ask for One (vv. 14–20)

**14** When you enter the land the LORD your God is giving you and have taken possession of it and settled in it, and you say, "We want to set a king over us like all the surrounding nations" — **15** be sure to appoint over yourself the king the LORD your God chooses. Appoint a fellow Israelite as your king — you are not permitted to set a foreigner over you, one who is not your fellow Israelite. **16** But he must not accumulate great numbers of horses for himself or make the people return to Egypt to get more horses, for the LORD has told you, "You are never to go back that way again." **17** He must not take many wives for himself, or his heart will be led astray. He must not accumulate silver and gold in great quantities.

**18** When he takes the throne of his kingdom, he must write for himself on a scroll a copy of this law, taken from the scroll kept by the Levitical priests. **19** He is to keep this scroll beside him and read from it every day of his life — so that he may learn to fear the LORD his God and carefully follow all the words of this law and these decrees, **20** so that he will not consider himself better than his fellow Israelites and will not turn from the law to the right or to the left. Then he and his descendants will reign for a long time over his kingdom in Israel.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — Connecting sacrifice to justice**: PNEUMA notes that this brief verse sits between the justice laws (ch. 16) and the apostate-trial laws (vv. 2–7). The connection is not accidental: defective offerings reflect defective worship, just as defective trials reflect defective justice. What you bring to God reveals the condition of your heart.

**v. 3 — Sun, moon, stars**: The "host of heaven" was the astral religion of Mesopotamia and Canaan — the worship of celestial bodies as divine powers. Deuteronomy is insistent: creation does not have divine authority. Only the Creator does.

**v. 6 — "Two or three witnesses"**: One witness is never sufficient to take a human life. This evidentiary standard — later cited in the New Testament for church discipline (Matt 18:16; 2 Cor 13:1) — is a systemic protection against wrongful execution. The state's coercive power to kill requires robust corroboration.

**v. 7 — "The hands of the witnesses must be the first"**: Requiring the accusers to personally execute the condemned raises the cost of false testimony to the maximum: you do not merely send someone to death — you must do it yourself. This is a brilliant deterrent against malicious accusation.

**vv. 8–13 — The Supreme Court**: PNEUMA highlights that this passage establishes a hierarchy of judicial authority. Local courts handle ordinary cases. Unresolvable hard cases go to the central sanctuary — where the priest and judge together adjudicate. The fusion of priestly and judicial authority signals that law in Israel is always theological: justice is administered before God, not merely before the state.

**v. 12 — Contempt for the court**: The death penalty for defying the court's ruling is not brutality for its own sake — it is protecting the integrity of a justice system that could otherwise unravel into tribal vengeance. Without binding authority, no court can function.

**vv. 14–20 — The Law of the King**: This is one of the most remarkable passages in the ancient world — a law about kings written before the monarchy existed, explicitly limiting royal power. PNEUMA notes three prohibitions: (1) no horse accumulation (military buildup), (2) no many wives (political alliances through marriage), (3) no silver and gold accumulation (personal wealth extraction). Solomon violated all three spectacularly (1 Kings 10–11), and Deuteronomy reads like a prophetic indictment of his reign written in advance.

**v. 16 — "You are never to go back that way again"**: The Exodus was not a return option. Egypt is the paradigm of bondage; returning there — physically, economically, or spiritually — is a return to slavery. Military horse-trading with Egypt was not merely a strategic bad idea but a symbolic betrayal of the Exodus identity.

**v. 18 — "Write for himself a copy of this law"**: Hebrew *mishneh ha-Torah* — a repetition/copy of the Torah. In later Greek this phrase became the title of the book: *Deuteronomy* (deuteros = second, nomos = law). The king's first act upon ascending the throne is to write out the Torah with his own hand. He is not above the law; he is formed by it.

**v. 19 — "Read from it every day of his life"**: Daily Torah reading is not a priestly or scholarly discipline — it is the king's discipline. Power is safe only in a person continually shaped by God's word. The king who stops reading stops being accountable to anything beyond his own will.

**v. 20 — "Not consider himself better than his fellow Israelites"**: Hebrew *l'vilti rum l'vavo me'echav* — that his heart not be lifted above his brothers. The king is a fellow Israelite, not a semi-divine ruler. Deuteronomy's vision of kingship is radically horizontal: one citizen entrusted with authority, not a sovereign beyond the law. — *PNEUMA*
